Aria's heart filled with compassion. She took her most precious possession—a cloak woven from threads of emerald, violet, and gold—and offered it to the Sky. "Wear this," she said, "and you will never be plain again."

The Sky accepted the gift gratefully. But as Aria descended the mountain, a fierce wind caught the cloak, tearing it into a thousand shimmering ribbons that scattered across the northern sky.

The Sky wept at the loss, but the tears only made the ribbons dance more brilliantly. From that night forward, the Aurora Borealis has painted the darkness with waves of ethereal color—a reminder that acts of kindness, even when broken, create beauty that endures forever.

Myth: Long ago, a lonely shepherd boy named Kael lived at the edge of the frozen north. Each night, he watched the dark sky and wished he could bring light to comfort the cold world below.

"I wish to give you a gift," Kael replied, "but I have nothing beautiful enough."

The Sky whispered, "Give me what matters most to you."

The Sky rejoiced, finally adorned with colors of its own. And Kael understood that his grandmother's light now belonged to everyone beneath the night sky.

Thus were born the Northern Lights—born from sacrifice, transformed by wonder, eternal in their beauty.
